The objective is to solve each level by moving and/or switching between selectable pieces so that every goal tile marked with a '+' is occupied by a selectable piece at the same time, within the per-level step budget, progressing through all levels until the engine reports WIN for the final level. Each action moves the currently selected piece on a grid where each cell corresponds to 3-pixel increments in the provided [x, y] coordinates. MOVE_* attempts to shift the selected piece one cell in that direction; movement can be stopped by walls/boundaries or by other objects, while some adjacent objects (blocks) can be pushed if they are directly in the movement direction and space/interaction rules allow the push. Levels may include multiple selectable pieces; you change which one you control by CLICKing using the exact pixel/grid coordinates listed for that piece in the state (same coordinate system as player.position and objects). The movement.{direction}_blocked flags are only hints about adjacency and do not guarantee the move will fail.

The game uses a grid-based movement system where: (1) CLICK on a position teleports/moves the selected piece to that location if it's reachable, (2) MOVE_LEFT/RIGHT/UP/DOWN moves the selected piece in that cardinal direction by a fixed distance (appears to be 3 units based on movement patterns), (3) Pieces can be blocked by obstacles or walls that prevent movement, (4) The selected piece can be moved between different positions to navigate around obstacles, (5) Each level has a step budget (in this case started at ~20+ steps and counted down to 0), (6) When steps reach 0, the run ends regardless of whether the goal was achieved. The game requires efficient path-planning to reach the goal within the step limit.

The objective is to navigate selectable game pieces on a grid and position pushable objects (blocks) onto goal tile(s) marked with '+' on the semantic grid. Each level has a specific success condition—typically getting at least one block onto a goal position, which triggers a WIN state. Movement operates on a pixel-based coordinate system where positions are [x, y] with 3 pixels per cell. The game features multiple mechanics: (1) **Selection**: Click with CLICK and target_position to switch control between different selectable pieces (marked 'P' for current, 'S' for others). (2) **Movement**: MOVE_LEFT/RIGHT/UP/DOWN attempt to shift the selected piece; if a pushable block (marked 'B') is adjacent in that direction, it gets pushed along. (3) **Walls**: Static obstacles (marked '#') block movement, but their exact blocking behavior must be discovered through interaction—not all walls behave identically. (4) **Hints vs Reality**: The movement_blocked heuristic only indicates adjacent obstacles but doesn't guarantee a move will fail—actual outcomes depend on object types and wall configuration. (5) **Resource Constraint**: Each level has a finite step budget; inefficient exploration depletes it quickly. The puzzle likely requires coordinating multiple pieces strategically to create a solution path.
